did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a hybrid deep learning model named MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE (combining Convolutional Neural Networks and Vision Transformer Encoder) for the automated classification and early diagnosis of skin cancer (specifically melanoma and squamous cell carcinoma) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'RESEARCH' in the journal header. It presents original experimental work, including the development of a new hybrid deep learning model (MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE), detailed methodology sections (Section 3), experimental results on the ISIC2019 and HAM10000 datasets (Section 4), ablation studies, and statistical significance analysis. While it includes a 'Related Work' section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical contributions and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: ViTE-MobileNetV2-ResNet101: Fusion Vision Transformer Encoder and CNNs Based on Spatial Detail Enhancement for Early Diagnosis Skin Cancer
journal: International Journal of Computational Intelligence Systems
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early skin cancer diagnosis by developing a hybrid deep learning model named MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE, which integrates Convolutional Neural Networks with a Vision Transformer Encoder enhanced by a Spatial Detail Enhancement Block. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC2019 dataset, which contains 25,331 dermoscopic images across seven clinically relevant classes. Performance assessment yielded an average Area Under the ROC Curve of 96.94% and a classification accuracy of 98%. The model demonstrated high sensitivity for malignant classes, achieving 98.5% for melanoma and 91.5% for squamous cell carcinoma, alongside an average specificity of 99.55% and precision of 94.44%. These results indicate that the proposed architecture effectively combines local feature extraction with global context modeling to improve the detection of skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the ISIC2019 dataset used for training and evaluation. 628 images in the full dataset. Reported sensitivity of 91.5% for the proposed hybrid model and 76.5% for Enhanced ViTE with SDEB block.

all_task_types.target: Seven clinically relevant skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Vascular Lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories using the ISIC2019 dataset. The model predicts class labels for skin cancer detection, with performance measured by accuracy, sensitivity (recall), specificity, precision, and AUC for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset for skin image analysis and computer-aided diagnosis of skin lesions, resulting from a joint effort among institutions including the Department of Dermatology, Medical University of Vienna, and Queensland Skin Cancer Clinic. It contains high-resolution dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training the proposed MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE hybrid model and baseline CNN models (MobileNetV2, ResNet101). The dataset was partitioned into a fixed split with 64% allocated to training.
use.validation: Used for validation during model development. The dataset was partitioned with 16% allocated to validation.
use.testing: Used for independent testing and performance evaluation of the proposed model and baselines. The dataset was partitioned with 20% allocated to testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the statistical significance of the difference in mean AUC between the proposed MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE model and baseline models (Optimized MobileNetV2, Optimized ResNet101, Enhanced ViTE with/without SDEB, ResNet101-ViTE, MobileNetV2-ViTE).
To generate a distribution of AUC values for each model comparison used in the statistical significance analysis.

To assess the stability and repeatability of the proposed model's performance by running the training and evaluation pipeline three times with independent random seeds (42, 72, 99) and reporting mean and standard deviation.
To determine statistical significance for the paired t-tests, using a conservative threshold of p < 0.01 due to multiple comparisons.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the mean Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) between the proposed MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE model and the baseline models.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the mean AUC between the proposed MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE model and the baseline models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The proposed model's performance was significantly better than all baseline models (Optimized MobileNetV2, Optimized ResNet101, Enhanced ViTE with/without SDEB, ResNet101-ViTE, MobileNetV2-ViTE) as all p-values were less than 0.001, which is below the significance threshold of 0.01.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE (Hybrid model combining Convolutional Neural Networks and Vision Transformer Encoder)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained MobileNetV2 and Pre-trained ResNet101 backbones were used as initial feature extractors before optimization.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images split into patches for ViTE; height H, width W, channels C for CNNs).

backbone_components: Enhanced Vision Transformer Encoder (ViTE) with Spatial Detail Enhancement Block (SDEB)

mobile_net_v2_optimization.modifications: First convolution layer (3x3, 32 filters) and first two bottleneck blocks retained. Bottlenecks 3-6 channel counts reduced by 25-75%. Bottlenecks 7-17 truncated. Global Average Pooling (GAP) used instead of flattening. Fully connected layers removed.

resnet101_optimization.modifications: First feature extraction layers (7x7, 64-filter convolutions) retained. Conv2_x and Conv3_x stages reduced to two blocks each. Conv4_x stage pruned from 23 to 3 blocks. Conv5_x stage kept in two blocks. Classification head replaced with Global Average Pooling.

vite_component.structure: Six blocks, each incorporating multi-head attention, layer normalization, and Spatial Detail Enhancement Block (SDEB).
vite_component.sdeb_details: Convolutional layers with varying kernel sizes (7x7, 5x5, 3x3) followed by a 1x1 convolution to mitigate spatial information loss from patch embedding.
vite_component.positional_embeddings: Linearly projected patches appended with positional embeddings.

architecture_details.fusion_mechanism: Feature vectors from MobileNetV2 and ResNet101 underwent dimensionality reduction via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and were flattened. These were merged with ViTE features. Cross-attention used for weight assignments across backbone pathways.
architecture_details.classification_head: Normalization block, flattening block, dropout block, and multilayer perceptron (MLP) layers.

training_details.augmentation: Rotation, translation, cropping, scaling, and flipping.
training_details.preprocessing: Adaptive Average Filter and Laplacian Filter for image enhancement; PCA for dimensionality reduction of CNN features.

preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement using Adaptive Average Filter for noise mitigation and edge preservation
Image enhancement using Laplacian Filter for edge structure and lesion contour enhancement
Fusion of adaptive filtered and Laplacian filtered outputs for final enhanced image
Data augmentation including rotation, translation, cropping, scaling, and flipping
Geometric transformations: horizontal and vertical flips, variable degree rotations, zoom-in and zoom-out
Photometric transformations: brightness adjustments, contrast modifications, and saturation enhancement
Class balancing via tailored augmentation factors to achieve near-parity across classes
Dimensionality reduction using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on CNN feature vectors
Splitting dermoscopic images into patches for Vision Transformer Encoder input
Linear projection of patches with positional embeddings

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal and vertical flips, rotations at variable degrees, and zoom-in and zoom-out.
Brightness adjustments, contrast modifications, and saturation enhancement.

Tailored augmentation factors applied exclusively to the training set to achieve near-parity across classes. Factors ranged from 1x (for the majority class NV) to 56x (for the minority class DF). The final balanced dataset contained approximately 8240–8880 samples per class.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid model, MobileNetV2-ResNet101-ViTE, achieved an average Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 96.94% and a classification accuracy of 98% on the ISIC2019 dataset, outperforming standalone CNNs and enhanced ViT models.
The model demonstrated high sensitivity for malignant classes, achieving 98.5% for melanoma and 91.5% for squamous cell carcinoma, which helps reduce false negatives in early diagnosis.
The architecture consistently reported high specificity (average 99.55%) and precision (average 94.44%), indicating strong capability in distinguishing malignant from benign lesions.

The integration of a Spatial Detail Enhancement Block (SDEB) with the Vision Transformer Encoder effectively mitigated spatial information loss during patch embedding, improving feature representation for fine-grained lesion patterns.

The hybrid approach successfully combined local feature extraction from optimized CNN backbones (MobileNetV2 and ResNet101) with global context modeling from the ViT, addressing limitations inherent in using either architecture alone.

The model showed significant improvements in previously challenging classes, such as Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma (AKIEC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), compared to baseline models.
Statistical analysis using paired t-tests confirmed that the performance difference between the proposed model and baseline models was significant (p < 0.001).

The study acknowledges that while the model performs well on major classes, challenges remain for minority classes like Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC), which had lower sensitivity scores (80.9% and 96.3% respectively in the proposed model, though lower in ablation variants), indicating potential limitations in handling rare or visually similar lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC2019 dataset for training and internal validation, with external validation performed only on the HAM10000 dataset; no other independent external cohorts were used to assess generalizability.

The model is purely image-based and explicitly lacks integration of clinical metadata (e.g., patient demographics, lesion evolution over time), which limits its clinical utility compared to multimodal diagnostic approaches.

Performance on minority classes remains a limitation; specifically, Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC) showed lower sensitivity (91.6% and 75.6% respectively on HAM10000) due to underrepresentation in datasets.

The paper acknowledges that misclassifications predominantly occur between lesion types with overlapping visual features, such as SCC vs AKIEC and MEL vs BKL, indicating inherent diagnostic ambiguity in these categories.

Class balancing was achieved via tailored augmentation factors (up to 56x for minority classes) on the training set only, which may introduce bias if the augmented distribution does not reflect true clinical prevalence.

The proposed model's high specificity (average 99.55%) suggests a strong capability to distinguish malignant from benign lesions, but the trade-off in sensitivity for rare classes highlights the risk of false negatives in those specific subtypes.
The study does not report computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) or deployment feasibility, despite claiming the model is 'scalable and accessible'.
